cards.text: To use std::string, include the header {{c1::<string>}}.
To read a full line of input including spaces, use {{c1::std::getline(std::cin, str)}}.
The member function {{c1::.length()}} or {{c1::.size()}} returns the number of characters in a std::string.
std::string objects can be concatenated with the {{c1::+}} or {{c2::+=}} operators.

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.explanation: The + operator concatenates two strings together.

cards.question: What's the problem with using std::cin to read a string with spaces?
cards.answer: std::cin only reads up to the first whitespace, so name would contain "John" only. Use std::getline() instead to read the full line.
cards.explanation: std::cin >> stops at whitespace. For full line input with spaces, use std::getline(std::cin, name).

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.explanation: .length() returns the number of characters in the string.

cards.front: What is the difference between std::cin and std::getline() for string input?
cards.back:
• std::cin >> str: reads until first whitespace
  • std::getline(std::cin, str): reads entire line including spaces until newline

cards.front: Why is std::string preferred over C-style strings (char arrays)?
cards.back:
• Automatic memory management (no manual allocation)
  • Safer (no buffer overflows)
  • Easier to use (operators like +, ==)
  • More features (.length(), .substr(), etc.)

cards.explanation:
std::string can be initialized and assigned in multiple ways:
  • Default initialization (empty string)
  • From string literal
  • From another string (copy)
  • Assignment after initialization
All memory management is automatic.
examples: std::string s3{ s2 };  // Copy from s2

cards.back: Standard library class for managing sequences of characters with automatic memory management
